title: Ships Caught in the Middle: US and Iran Seize Vessels, Breaking Rules
summary:
Imagine you're a sailor just doing your job, delivering goods across the ocean. Then suddenly, your ship is captured by a country's military. That's what's happening to sailors right now — and a major shipping organization says it's against the rules.

The International Chamber of Shipping, which represents about 80% of the world's merchant ships, says both the United States and Iran are violating international law (the rules countries agree to follow). Iran has seized two ships, and the US has captured one. The organization says the sailors are innocent people caught in a political fight.

John Stawpert, a director at the organization, said, "These are innocent farers and they should be allowed to go about their jobs without fear of imprisonment."

The trouble is happening near the Strait of Hormuz, a narrow waterway that about one-fifth of the world's oil passes through. Iran has threatened to charge tolls there, which the shipping group says has no basis in law. The US has also set up a naval blockade (a military barrier) around Iranian ports.

Because of all this, about 20,000 seafarers are stuck on ships in the Gulf. Their food and mental health are becoming serious concerns. And fuel prices are going up worldwide because oil can't get through easily.

The shipping group is calling on both countries to let the ships go and respect freedom of navigation — the right of ships to travel freely.
why_it_matters: This matters because it shows how fights between countries can affect everyday things like the price of fuel and the safety of workers on ships.

keywords.explanation: Taken by force, like when a military takes a ship.
Rules that most countries agree to follow.
A narrow waterway in the Middle East where many oil ships travel.
A military barrier that stops ships from entering an area.
The right of ships to travel freely in international waters.
Sailors or people who work on ships.

questions.question: What does the International Chamber of Shipping say about the US and Iran?
options: They are making a new trade deal

questions.question: How many ships have been seized by Iran?
How many ships have been seized by the US?
What important waterway is near where the seizures happen?
About how many seafarers are stuck on ships in the Gulf?
What problem is happening to fuel prices because of this?

background_read: The Strait of Hormuz is a very important waterway because many oil tankers pass through it.
When countries argue, they sometimes stop ships, which can affect fuel prices around the world.
The International Chamber of Shipping is a group that speaks for shipping companies and works to protect sailors' rights.
Article_Structure: WHO: Sailors and shipping companies (represented by the International Chamber of Shipping); the US and Iran.
WHAT: Both countries are seizing ships, violating international law, and causing problems for sailors and oil prices.
WHERE: Near the Strait of Hormuz, in the Gulf.
WHY: Because of a political conflict between the US and Iran; the sailors are innocent victims.

perspectives.description: The shipping group is worried about the safety of sailors and wants the ships released so trade can flow normally.
If oil cannot pass through, fuel prices go up, which can affect everyone who uses cars or products shipped by sea.
Thousands of sailors are stuck on ships with limited food and stress, which is a serious health risk.
